result:
The hour before the sun remembers the city is when the world speaks in its smallest voices. A radiator clicks like a beetle in a jar. The refrigerator hum shifts keys as if it’s practicing scales. Down on the street, a delivery truck exhales, and a man laughs alone, one bright pulse of sound that flutters up between buildings and perches on my windowsill for a breath before flying off. Somewhere a dog shakes its name tag against a bowl. The air smells faintly of warm pennies and last night’s rain.

I make coffee and stand by the window, watching a traffic light change for no one. There is a comfort in systems that keep working when nobody’s watching. Lights blink on time. Yeast rises overnight. The sea keeps filling the moon’s pockets and emptying them again. Inside my chest, the small metronome does its work. I have always loved the way the ordinary insists on itself, how the banal is a faithful dog, returning with the newspaper of another minute, another, another.

I once knew a clockmaker who claimed his fingers felt the hours. He kept old faces stacked like plates, hands rusted blue, cracked enamel dials with hairline fractures like rivers seen from an airplane. He would hold a gear to his ear the way people hold shells, listening for the sea inside the teeth. “They want to keep going,” he said, “but not all in the same direction.” He died one spring while the lilacs were thick in the alley. His shop sat with all its stopped heartbeats for weeks, dust negotiating truce with sunbeams.

At the bus stop, a child tries to name the clouds and gets halfway through a list invented on the spot—pillow clouds, birthday cake clouds, gray-sock clouds—before his mother says, gently, you can’t name everything. An older man in a suit with a frayed cuff is ripping his newspaper into tiny strips and putting them in his pocket. We must all have our rituals. A woman with a bicycle eats a plum with care, leaning forward so the juice falls toward the curb and not her shirt. Her hair catches light like a blade.

Once I kept a stone in my pocket for a month. It had a fossil in it, the suggestion of a fern that had pressed itself into the mud and left its memory for hands ten thousand winters later. I liked to rub the fossil with my thumb when I had to talk to someone important. My thumb learned the small curls, the stubbornness of a leaf that would not be entirely pressed out of the rock. I became superstitious about it, wouldn’t go to the bank without the stone, or to the office where the indoor plants leaned toward a window with such quiet hunger. When I lost it in the back of a taxi, the panic surprised me as much as the tenderness. It is a strange discipline, losing gently.

In the thrift store there is an aisle of remote controls nobody knows how to match. They are a city of gray islands, each with a different topography of buttons. In a basket nearby, a tangled congregation of laces and belts is pretending to be a storm. I buy a honey-colored sweater with a single cigarette burn where the left elbow would have leaned out of a window. Someone was held by this sweater on evenings when the sky was the color of wet envelope glue. I pay in coins, a sound like small rain.

If you open the back of a radio that was built before I was born, it looks like a neighborhood. There are cul-de-sacs of solder, bright rows like porches where capacitors have been sitting forever on summer evenings. There are alleys of wire that have never known dust. My father had one of these on the kitchen table for weeks, a set from a yard sale that promised “works if you shake it.” He removed the back and smiled at its tiny city. I held the bowl of screws like communion. Sometimes, even now, when the weather changes, I can smell the soldering iron, something tinny and evergreen in my sinuses.

On the day the council cut down the big elm on the corner, there were people on the sidewalk who had not spoken to one another in a decade. We gathered like gulls after the trawler leaves, drawn by the noisy wound. A woman in a turquoise sweater pressed her hand to her mouth. A man in paint-spattered jeans took photographs as if evidence could alter the chain’s appetite. Once the tree lay on its side, the rings were wide and uneven, secrets sliced into commas. A girl counted them with her finger and kept losing her place. I thought of the clockmaker and his claim that wood remembers with its whole body.

The neighbor who plays the trumpet in the afternoons has switched to the flugelhorn. It is a softer voice, rounder, like whispering into a loaf of bread. He clambers up and down scales with the hopeful persistence of a bird testing branches after ice. Some days, he will land on a melody so sweet that I put my pen down and let it blow a window in my mind. Other days, he stalls and sputters, and I make tea to outlast him. We are, most of us, practicing all the time.

Walking home at dusk, I find a library book on the stoop of the closed bodega. It has a pressed leaf inside, gold and brittle, a little map of its own past roads. In the margin of page 138 someone has written “No,” and on page 172, “Yes,” and I wonder at the argument that happened here between the living and the text. I return the book the next morning and think of the unseen chain tug I’ve made, the tiny ledger of borrowings and returns that keep us from flying apart entirely.

Sometimes, in the hour the radio hosts like to call drive time, I imagine a child who does not yet exist asking me what we did with our time. I want to say we made things, we listened well, we kissed on stairwells, we walked on bridges and did not jump. I want to say we learned to be kind not because we were rewarded but because we could not stand ourselves otherwise. I want to say we planted rosemary in cracked parking lots and whispered to each other in line at the post office when the lights flickered. I want to say we tried.

When the storm finally comes, it arrives not as a disaster but as an equalizer. The expensive umbrellas invert like humble mouths. The pigeons go bulbous and stand with their heads tucked, pretending to be stones. The gutters become silver hurries. By the river, the tide pretends it has won. On the bridge there is a couple laughing so hard the rain cannot land on them. I am soaked through the shoulders and do not care. Water has a memory for laughter, I decide; it will carry this cackling into the drains and back to the sea, where a fish will feel a sudden urge to be ridiculous and leap.

At night, the city exhales the day like someone who’s been holding their breath a bit too long. Windows turn into television sets of unknown lives. A woman at a desk rubs her eyes; a cat mirrors a man stretching; a plant is the only witness to a jar of marbles being opened and poured, like rain in reverse. The trumpet neighbor plays one tender note and it hangs between us like a string you could walk along if you didn’t look down. I think of the clockmaker, of the radio’s small city, of the fossil fern, of the child naming the unnameable sky, of the tree rings’ commas saying, wait, wait, there’s more.

Sleep comes like a ferry. You line up, you file aboard, the engine shudders, you drift from shore, you stand at the rail and watch lights become sequins and then stars. You hold the railing with teeth marks in it and wonder about the mouths that made them. You close your eyes and, for a few seconds, float perfectly between places, as if such a balance might be sustained. For those seconds, everything is enough. Then the horn, low as a whale’s patience, and the dark water answers by simply agreeing.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
